rationale: The cited paper is used in the theory-development and discussion sections as part of the prior stream on how consumers infer others' preferences when information is absent or unclear. The citing paper then explicitly positions its contribution against that stream by arguing that an active no-preference message is different from simple noncommunication and produces distinct downstream effects. This is more than a passing background mention because it helps define the conceptual contrast that motivates the new hypotheses and additional comparison tests.
relation_description: The citing paper builds on prior work including Wu et al. (2021) to argue that no-preference communication is a distinct case from absent preference information. It extends that line by testing how this communication triggers perceived undisclosed preferences and harms joint decision difficulty, social evaluations, and choice outcomes.
